steps.prompt: How many verbs are in this sentence?

hints.One: Look again — both 'Odi' and 'amo' are finite verbs joined by 'et'
hints.Three: 'et' is a conjunction, not a verb — only 'Odi' and 'amo' are verbs
hints.None: Both 'Odi' and 'amo' are 1st person singular verbs

hints.to smell: 'to smell' is 'olere' — 'Odi' comes from the defective verb 'odisse'

steps.target: It is a defective verb — perfect in form but present in meaning
choices: It is a defective verb — perfect in form but present in meaning

hints.It is a deponent verb: Deponent verbs are passive in form, active in meaning — 'Odi' is perfect in form, present in meaning
hints.It is a subjunctive form: Subjunctive of a regular verb would look different — 'Odi' is indicative perfect with present sense
hints.It is an archaic spelling: 'Odi' is not an archaic form — it's a defective verb like 'memini' and 'coepi'

hints.amo, amare, amavi, amatum: These are the principal parts of 'amare' (to love) — the other verb in this poem
hints.audio, audire, audivi, auditum: These are the principal parts of 'audire' (to hear)
hints.memini, meminisse, —: These are the principal parts of 'meminisse' (to remember) — another defective verb, but not this one

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect would be 'amabam' — 'amo' is the simple present 1st person form
hints.Perfect: Perfect would be 'amavi' — 'amo' uses the present stem
hints.Future: Future would be 'amabo' — 'amo' is present indicative

hints.odi, odisse, osurus: These are the principal parts of 'odisse' (to hate) — the other verb in this poem
hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the principal parts of 'habere' (to have)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the principal parts of 'dare' (to give)

hints.Preposition: Prepositions govern nouns — 'et' joins the two verbs 'Odi' and 'amo'
hints.Adverb: 'et' can sometimes mean 'also', but here it coordinates two verbs
hints.Interjection: Interjections are exclamations — 'et' is a coordinating conjunction
